ADHD v6.5
About this tool

Hello and thanks for using my ADHD assessment generator/guide.

Use this however suits you. Treat it as a structured guide with prompts, fill it all out and let it flag any safety concerns, or jump straight to the bits you need.

  • Generate a GP letter or a plain-text output ready to drop into your AI scribe.
  • Build a patient letter with psychoeducation tailored to the assessment.
  • Use the titration plan builder for stimulant and non-stimulant regimens, with auto-generated scripts.
  • Open the icons up the top for state-specific prescribing requirements, MBS billing, example titration regimens, and GP handover templates.

Have fun!

    How to Use This Tool

    How to Use This Tool

    Purpose: A structured clinical companion for ADHD assessments and follow-up reviews — not a replacement for clinical judgment. Capture the consult as you go, and leave with the report, GP letter, patient document, eScript text, and titration plan ready to send.

    Quick orientation — the layout

    The two strips at the top of every page

    • Reference ribbon (icons): 🦮 Guide · ✍️ Prescribing · 💊 Titration · 💵 Billing · 📅 Follow-up · 🤝 Handover. These open standalone reference overlays on top of whatever you're working on — nothing in your form is lost.
    • Section nav (two rows of tabs): the slate row is your assessment workflow (Psych Hx · Medical Hx · Alerts), the dark green row is your planning workflow (EDU · Med Plan · Follow-up · Reports). Both rows stay pinned as you scroll. Click any tab to jump straight to that section.

    Tab badges

    • A red number on Psych Hx, Medical Hx or Alerts shows the current safety/gap count for that section. You can see at a glance whether anything needs your attention.
    How to use it

    During the consult

    • Tap items as the patient describes them. The form follows the natural arc of an assessment, so you can fill from the top down.
    • Nothing is compulsory — fill what's relevant and skip the rest.
    • The form auto-saves to your browser as you type. Reload-safe.

    After the consult

    • Fill in from memory or scribe notes, then jump to Reports to generate everything at once.

    Hybrid

    • Pre-fill demographics before the patient arrives, fill clinical sections during, finish documents after.
    Top of page — Session info

    What lives here

    • Psychiatrist name and qualifications (saved between sessions — only enter once).
    • Visit date (auto-fills to today).
    • Patient name, age, gender, GP.
    • MBS item picker, with 291/92435 eligibility check (auto-flags if you've billed one in the last 12 months).
    • Review type: Initial · Follow-up · Titration · Script — drives the rest of the form (see "Follow-up reviews" below).
    Psych Hx tab

    Presenting concerns

    • Tap the difficulties the patient describes — single-concept tiles (no slashes), so you only tick what's actually endorsed.
    • Free-text "presenting notes" box for anything else.

    Pre-screening & collateral

    • Each item is a self-contained tile: Patient ASRS, DIVA-5 (with separate symptom counts), other screening; then collateral — Observer ASRS, GP referral, school reports, psych report, Vanderbilt parents/teacher, plus an Other collateral textarea.
    • Items marked Not provided automatically tick the matching chip in "Patient to provide" under Follow-up — you can untick manually if you don't want it actioned.

    Developmental history

    • Each ? button reveals interview prompts. Tap items the patient endorses — inattention and H/I symptoms are tracked separately and counted (N/9).
    • Once 5+ symptoms in either domain, Criterion A auto-checks. Childhood / cross-situational / impairment items auto-fill Criteria B, C and D.

    Differential diagnoses

    • All default to blank. Tap Nil / Past / Current for each.
    • When Past or Current is selected, an inline detail box appears so you can record specifics (e.g. "mild, last episode 2yrs ago"). The detail flows into the letter as the parenthetical.
    • Flagged differentials (bipolar, schizophrenia, suicidality, EDs, ABI, substance-induced, neurodegen, medical) turn red when Current and trigger an Alerts entry.
    • Criterion E auto-unchecks if any Current differential is set; you can manually re-check.

    Substance use

    • Nil / Past / Curr per substance. When Current or Past is selected an inline detail box appears (e.g. "daily ~1g", "weekly ~point") — the detail appears in the letter parenthetical.

    Family psychiatric history

    • Nil / Present per category. When Present is selected an inline detail box appears for relation/severity (e.g. "Mother, hospitalised x2") — appears in the letter parenthetical.

    Mental state examination

    • Tap the descriptors that apply per MSE section. The letter renders these as flowing prose.

    DSM-5-TR criteria + Diagnosis

    • DSM criteria auto-tick as you fill the developmental section.
    • Diagnosis (Yes / No / Insufficient / Deferred) and subtype auto-set when criteria are met.
    • Subtype is now clearable — re-click the active subtype to deselect it. Setting dx to anything other than Yes also auto-clears the subtype.
    • Manual overrides stick. "Deselect all" resets so auto-logic can take over again.
    Medical Hx tab

    Current medications, allergies, medical conditions

    • Free-text current meds + allergies; medical-conditions checklist with ? buttons explaining prescribing implications.

    Cardiac screening

    • Each item flagged "?" has a clinical note. Flagged items (MAOI, hypertension, structural heart disease, etc.) bubble up to the Alerts tab.

    Investigations

    • Height + weight auto-calculate BMI (red-highlighted if <18.5).
    • BP entered as separate systolic + diastolic fields; HR as bpm.
    • BP ≥140/90, HR ≥100, or BMI <18.5 raise a Safety alert in the letter.
    • ECG and UDS as Normal/Abnormal/Not done buttons. "Not done" auto-ticks the matching chip in Follow-up "Patient to provide".

    SafeScript / RTPM

    • Record Checked (no concerns) / Concern / Not checked. "Not checked" raises a gap. "Concern" raises a Safety alert in the letter.
    Alerts tab
    • Flags — live-updated safety/clinical alerts (cardiac, suicidality, SafeScript, current substances, low BMI, billing conflicts, …).
    • Gaps — outstanding items before the assessment is complete (MSE not done, no follow-up set, DSM criteria incomplete, …).
    EDU tab — patient education
    • Tick the topics you discussed. Items in the "Non-pharmacological Strategies" and "Products and Resources" groups render as Non-pharmacological recommendations in the letter; everything else renders as Psychoeducation provided.
    • The full content of each topic also appears in the patient handout.
    Med Plan tab — titration + scripts

    Picking a med

    • Tap a medication tile to choose between Titration (multi-step) or Regular (single ongoing dose).
    • Pre-built protocols for each med (e.g. dex BD normal/fast/slow/TDS, Vyvanse 30+20, Vyvanse water method, Concerta etc.) load with one tap.
    • Or "+ Add medication manually" — picks a med, seeds it with a default morning dose, and lets you build the plan from scratch.

    Editing a step

    • Each step row: duration · time · stepper for tabs/caps · strength · ✎ free-text override · PRN · ×.
    • "+ Add step below" inserts a new step right after the one you clicked (not at the end).
    • Default duration is "1 week" (you can change to days).
    • The ✎ pencil flips a dose to free-text mode so you can type ranges like "0.5–1 tab (2.5–5mg)". ↺ flips back.
    • If a step has more than one timing, a "Total/day" chip appears.

    Scripts provided today

    • One script per distinct strength used in titration (so Vyvanse 20mg + 30mg makes two scripts automatically).
    • Repeats default to blank with a yellow "needs filling" border the first time you use a med — your choice is then remembered for that med across sessions.
    • The × is replaced by an Included / Excluded toggle. Excluded scripts are dimmed and skipped from outputs.
    • The dose textarea auto-derives from the titration plan and updates live; type to override (a 🔒 marker appears so picking a different formulation style won't overwrite your edit).
    • Copy instructions / Copy full script on each row, plus copy buttons in the Titration overlay.
    Follow-up tab

    Quick templates

    • One-tap templates: 4-wk titration, 6-wk titration, 3-mo stable, 6-mo stable, GP handover, Shared care.

    Quick add appointment

    • Four taps: With (Me / Your GP / Other) · In (1–12) · Unit (Days / Weeks / Months) · Length (15 / 30 / 45 / 60 min). A live preview shows the line that will be added.
    • "Me" resolves to your psychiatrist name. "Your GP" resolves to the GP name from Session info.

    Patient to provide / Monitoring / Safety scenarios

    • 2-column tile checkboxes — selected tiles turn green (red for the warn items: chest pain, crisis).
    • "Patient to provide" auto-ticks when the matching screening or investigation item was marked Not provided / Not done — manual unticks are preserved.
    • Safety scenarios drive both the patient document content and the Safety section of the letter.
    Reports tab — what gets generated

    Two views

    • Structured — sectioned report with headings, lists and tables.
    • Letter (291/92435) — flowing prose letter to the GP in serif typography, with a formal RE: / date letterhead, the diagnosis callout, the formulation paragraph, management plan with sub-headings, and a sign-off.

    Formulation styles

    • Three pill buttons above the Formulation textarea: Clinical · Narrative · Functional. Each generator pulls live state and writes a paragraph for you.
    • The text is editable — once you type, an "✎ edited" warning appears so picking another style doesn't silently overwrite your edit.
    • The formulation auto-regenerates if you change the diagnosis or other dependent fields (unless you've manually edited it).

    Diagnosis callout

    • Green ✓ box for confirmed ADHD; amber for "criteria not met"; indigo for insufficient/deferred.

    Safety section

    • Each safety concern (cardiac flag, low BMI, BP ≥140/90, HR ≥100, SafeScript concern, positive UDS, active SI, plus the user's selected safety scenarios) appears as a concern → recommended action bullet.

    Outputs

    • Copy (rich) · Copy plain text · Download .docx. The .docx filename is ADHD_Letter_<Patient>.docx or ADHD_Report_<Patient>.docx depending on which view is active.
    • Below the report there's a separate Patient document builder (cover letter + plan + monitoring + safety + handout + optional medical certificate / travel / workplace / study / driving letters).
    • And a Heidi / AI scribe context block — plain text dump of everything entered, ready to paste into Heidi, MedicalDirector, Best Practice, Dragon, etc.
    Reference overlays (icons in the top ribbon)
    • 🦮 Guide — this page.
    • ✍️ Prescribing — state-by-state stimulant authority requirements, dose limits, RTPM and UDS rules. Always available, even mid-form.
    • 💊 Titration — every titration protocol as a copyable card, plus a Treatment Rationale builder (consent / cardiac / contras / line / class / duration / plan) with a "Copy rationale" button. The rationale is reference-only — it doesn't leak into your reports.
    • 💵 Billing — MBS item dropdown grouped by modality with current rebate values.
    • 📅 Follow-up — follow-up styles guide.
    • 🤝 Handover — GP handover letter template + the full state-by-state continuation prescriber guide. Copy buttons under the letter, the guide, and a combined "Copy letter + guide".
    Follow-up / Titration / Script reviews
    • Set the review type at the top of the page (Initial / Follow-up / Titration / Script).
    • On non-Initial reviews, all the assessment cards stay in the form but pre-collapsed — expand any to update something. Investigations is collapsed by default; SafeScript stays expanded (must be checked every visit).
    • The Follow-Up Plan card jumps to the top of the page right after Session info, so it's the first thing you see.
    • The Reports letter opening adapts: "I saw [name] on [date] for a follow-up review. The outcome and updated management plan are summarised below." (or titration / script review).
    Tips & shortcuts

    General

    • ? buttons — interview prompts and clinical notes for any item.
    • Select all / Deselect all — bulk actions on every checklist.
    • All nil — sets every differential or substance to Nil in one tap.
    • Tab badges — red counts on Psych Hx, Medical Hx, Alerts.
    • Cards collapse by tapping their header (the chevron). Useful when a section is irrelevant.

    Mobile

    • The form is touch-friendly: 32–38px buttons, the dose stepper has clear tap targets, and the chip groups collapse to single column under 380px.
    • Reference overlays scroll independently so the reading position is preserved.
    • If the page looks stale after an update, append ?v=<n> to the URL (any number) to bypass Safari's cache.

    Privacy

    • Everything stays in your browser — no data is sent anywhere.
    • The form auto-saves to local storage as you type.
    • Use Reset (top right) to clear between patients.
    Suggested timing — staying within 50–60 minutes

    Sample allocation

    • 0–5 min — Session info, presenting concerns, reason for referral.
    • 5–10 min — Pre-screening & collateral (what the GP / patient has already done).
    • 10–20 min — Developmental history. Tick core symptoms as the patient describes them.
    • 20–30 min — Differentials, substance use, family history.
    • 30–35 min — Mental state examination, DSM criteria review (mostly auto-filled by now).
    • 35–40 min — Medical history, cardiac, investigations, SafeScript.
    • 40–50 min — Medication selection, titration plan, follow-up, scripts.
    • 50–60 min — Education, formulation style, generate the letter and patient document.

    Tips to stay on time

    • Use the ? prompts when you're unsure how to phrase an interview question — they're fast.
    • Differentials default to blank; only flip what's relevant.
    • For continuation patients, switch review type to Follow-up — the assessment cards collapse and Follow-Up Plan jumps to the top.
    📋 Prescriber Guide — Stimulant Authorities

    📋 Prescriber Guide — Stimulant Authorities in Australia

    There are two separate authorities when prescribing ADHD stimulants: (1) State or territory authority to legally prescribe stimulants, and (2) PBS authority for subsidy — to make it cheap.

    State-by-state

    Select a state for full prescribing authority requirements, dose limits, RTPM obligations, and when individual patient authority is needed.

    ACT

    Pre-Auth Criteria (Psychiatrists)

    • ADHD only
    • Not drug dependent
    • Within state dose limits
    • Electronic prescription only
    • Minimum age: 4

    Max Doses

    • Dex 40mg/day
    • MPH 60mg/day
    • MPH LA 72mg/day
    • Lisdex 70mg/day

    RTPM

    • Canberra Script — Recommended

    UDS Required?

    • No

    Individual Authority Required When…

    • Patient drug-dependent
    • Dose exceeds Standing Approval limits
    • Indication other than ADHD
    • Other non-routine / higher-risk circumstance

    Follow-up

    • Specialist review usually required for ongoing GP continuation

    Other Prescribers

    • Paediatricians and neurologists also prescribe under ACT Standing CHO Approval
    • GP continuation has separate notification requirements
    NSW

    Pre-Auth Criteria (Psychiatrists)

    • ADHD only
    • Not drug dependent
    • Within state dose limits
    • Minimum age: 4
    • Write CA2023 on every script

    Max Doses

    • Dex 50mg/day
    • MPH 108mg/day
    • Lisdex 70mg/day

    RTPM

    • SafeScript NSW — Recommended*
    • *Mandatory for GP continuation prescribers

    UDS Required?

    • No

    Individual Authority Required When…

    • Patient drug-dependent
    • Dose exceeds CA2023 limits
    • Indication other than ADHD

    Follow-up

    • Specialist review expected annually for ages 0–17
    • Adults often reviewed at longer intervals

    Other Prescribers

    • Paediatricians and neurologists also prescribe under class authority
    • GP continuation and initiation are separate NSW reform pathways
    NT

    Pre-Auth Criteria (Psychiatrists)

    • ADHD only
    • Not drug dependent
    • Within state dose limits
    • Minimum age: 4
    • Individual approval is the standard NT pathway

    Max Doses

    • Dex 60mg/day
    • MPH 80mg/day
    • Lisdex 70mg/day

    RTPM

    • NTScript — MANDATORY

    UDS Required?

    • No

    Individual Authority Required When…

    • Every routine patient goes through individual approval
    • Dose above published limits
    • Suspected misuse / complex case

    Follow-up

    • Specialist review within 2 years

    Other Prescribers

    • Paediatricians, neurologists, relevant physicians use NT approval pathway
    • GPs do not have broad independent class approval
    QLD

    Pre-Auth Criteria (Psychiatrists)

    • ADHD (age 0+)
    • Brain injury (age 4–17 only)
    • Narcolepsy (any age)
    • Continuing prior treatment
    • Not drug dependent
    • Within state dose limits
    • Source

    Max Doses

    • Dex 40mg/day
    • MPH 80mg/day
    • Lisdex 70mg/day

    RTPM

    • QScript — MANDATORY

    UDS Required?

    • No

    Individual Authority Required When…

    • Dose above psychiatrist limits
    • Patient drug-dependent or history of stimulant psychosis
    • Brain injury in patient aged 18+
    • Any other diagnosis outside authorised scope

    Follow-up

    • Specialist review every 2–3 years

    Other Prescribers

    • Paediatricians have their own paediatric scope
    • Specialist GPs (FRACGP/FACRRM) have adult ADHD pathway from Dec 2025
    SA

    Pre-Auth Criteria (Psychiatrists)

    • ADHD and other indications within psychiatric scope
    • Not drug dependent and nil schizophrenia (2nd opinion required)
    • No legislated dose limit
    • Any doctor can prescribe stimulants up to 2 months
    • Section 18A authority required if treatment continues beyond 2 months (via ScriptCheckSA)
    • Source

    Max Doses

    • No fixed max; second opinion may be required for unusually high doses

    RTPM

    • ScriptCheckSA — MANDATORY

    UDS Required?

    • No

    Individual Authority Required When…

    • Treatment continuing beyond 2 months without Section 18A authority
    • Drug-dependent patient
    • Unusually high or non-standard doses
    • Complex, off-label, or higher-risk situations

    Follow-up

    • Specialist review within 5 years (mandatory for re-authorisation)

    Other Prescribers

    • Paediatricians have their own role in shared-care pathways
    • GPs require separate shared-care / authority arrangements
    TAS

    Pre-Auth Criteria (Psychiatrists)

    • ADHD only
    • Not drug dependent
    • No legislated dose limit
    • Tasmanian psychiatrists need S59E approval
    • Interstate psychiatrists can prescribe without specific TAS approval

    Max Doses

    • Not regulated; set by specialist; may be subject to Panel review

    RTPM

    • TasScript — MANDATORY

    UDS Required?

    • No

    Individual Authority Required When…

    • Tasmanian psychiatrist without S59E approval
    • Drug-dependent patient
    • Non-ADHD indication
    • Unusually high dose / Panel review case

    Follow-up

    • Specialist review within 3 years

    Other Prescribers

    • Paediatricians follow same TAS framework
    • GPs need their own S59E pathway
    VIC

    Pre-Auth Criteria (Psychiatrists)

    • ADHD only
    • Not drug dependent
    • No fixed max dose

    Max Doses

    • No fixed maximum

    RTPM

    • SafeScript VIC — MANDATORY

    UDS Required?

    • No

    Individual Authority Required When…

    • Patient drug-dependent
    • Indication outside usual specialist scope
    • Other non-routine / high-risk situations

    Follow-up

    • Specialist review within 2 years for GP continuation (mandatory to renew GP prescribing permit)

    Other Prescribers

    • Paediatricians have VIC specialist exemption
    • Narcolepsy: respiratory and sleep physicians only
    • GPs and other non-specialists require Schedule 8 permits
    WA

    Pre-Auth Criteria (Psychiatrists)

    • ADHD, acquired brain injury, depression, binge eating disorder (source)
    • Not drug dependent
    • Within state dose limits
    • Minimum age: 4

    Max Doses

    • Dex 60mg/day
    • MPH 120mg/day
    • Lisdex 70mg/day

    RTPM

    • ScriptCheckWA — MANDATORY (from Jun 2025)

    UDS Required?

    • YES — age 16+ before commencing

    Individual Authority Required When…

    • Comorbid substance misuse within last 5 years
    • History of stimulant-induced psychosis, psychosis, or bipolar disorder
    • Dose exceeds code maximums
    • Condition outside psychiatrist-approved code indications

    Follow-up

    • Mandatory within 1 year for under 18s; adults up to 3 years

    Other Prescribers

    • Paediatricians, neurologists, rehab physicians, sleep physicians have separate code-defined scopes
    • Narcolepsy: neurologists, paediatric neurologists, respiratory and sleep physicians only
    • GP shared care follows separate WA arrangements

    Pre-Approved Indications by State

    Which states allow psychiatrists to prescribe without individual patient authority, by diagnosis:

    IndicationStates pre-approved for psychiatrists (no individual authority needed)
    ADHDACT, NSW, QLD, SA*, TAS, VIC, WA, within dose limits and drug-dependent criteria
    NarcolepsyQLD (any age), SA*
    Acquired brain injuryQLD (age 4–17 only), SA*, WA (any age)
    DepressionSA*, WA
    Binge eating disorderWA
    NT — ADHD, narcolepsy, idiopathic hypersomnia, and any other indicationIndividual patient authority required for every patient regardless of diagnosis

    * SA: any doctor can prescribe stimulants for up to 2 months without authority. For ongoing prescribing beyond 2 months, Section 18A approval is required via ScriptCheckSA.

    PBS Authority — How to Get It

    Go to PRODA → HPOS → My Providers → Authorities → Request Authority. You need your authority script authorisation number (next to your prescriber number on eScripts). You need a new authority for every new script, even if you're represcribing a script with previous authorisation.

    In the Authority Request, enter the total tablets per day and the total repeats you'll actually prescribe. Vyvanse needs individual PRODA authority for each capsule strength.

    For patients on 5+ tablets/day of dex IR or methylphenidate IR, the 20-day rule means they may run out. Enter 200 tablets (not the default 100) to get authority for 2 bottles. 200 is the maximum for methylphenidate; dex has no legal limit but be careful above 200.

    Private scripts: You can write a private script for any of these medications provided you have state pre-approval or individual state authorisation — regardless of whether the medication is on the PBS. PBS authority only determines whether the patient gets it subsidised. For example, if you obtain state approval to prescribe Vyvanse for binge eating disorder, you must write a private script because Vyvanse is only PBS-listed for ADHD.

    Authority Requirements & Private Costs (Chemist Warehouse)

    MedicationAuthorityPrivate Scirpt Cost
    Any authority script—$25
    Lisdexamfetamine (Vyvanse)PRODA required$30-$35
    Methylphenidate IR (Artige/Ritalin IR)PRODA required$25.99
    Ritalin LA 40mgPRODA required$42.99
    Ritalin LA 60mgPRODA required$56.99
    Concerta 36mgPRODA (Dx as child)$47.99
    Concerta 54mgPRODA (Dx as child)$56.99
    Atomoxetine (Strattera)Streamline 7876 / 7890 (Dx as child)$35.99
    Guanfacine (Intuniv)Streamlined (Dx as child)$58.99
    Clonidine (Catapres)Not PBS listed for ADHD$19.99

    Note: Prices at Chemist Warehouse. Authority script makes it $25 per script (as of Jan 2026).

    📋 Titration Protocols

    Go-to titration schedules — modify as needed based on clinical response and tolerability.

    Stimulants — Amphetamine-based

    Dexamphetamine IR (5mg tabs)
    PhaseDurationDosingNote
    Days 1–22 daysMorning: 2.5mg · Midday: 2.5mg
    Days 3–97 daysMorning: 5mg · Midday: 5mgIf tolerating, continue.
    Days 10–167 daysMorning: 10mg · Midday: 5mgIf tolerating but not fully effective, increase.
    Ongoing—Morning: 10mg · Midday: 10mgAdjust at review. Consider afternoon 5–10mg if needed.
    Vyvanse 30+20mg No requirement to start with short-acting dex first — titrate Vyvanse directly
    PhaseDurationDosingNote
    Week 17 daysMorning: 30mg capsule
    Weeks 2–314 daysMorning: 30mg + 20mg (50mg total)If tolerating well, continue.
    Week 4+ if needed—Morning: 30mg + 20mg + 20mg (70mg total)Only if tolerating 50mg but still symptomatic.
    Vyvanse 30mg → 50mg (simple)
    PhaseDurationDosingNote
    Weeks 1–321 daysMorning: 30mg capsule
    Ongoing—Morning: 50mg capsuleOnly if tolerating 30mg but still symptomatic.
    Vyvanse — Water Dissolution Method

    Dissolve 70mg capsule in 70mL water (1mg/mL). Draw prescribed mL, drink immediately, discard rest. Switch to capsule once stable dose found.

    PhaseDurationDose (mL = mg)Note
    Week 17 daysMorning: 30mL (30mg)
    Week 27 daysMorning: 40mL (40mg)If tolerating, continue increasing.
    Week 37 daysMorning: 50mL (50mg)
    Week 47 daysMorning: 60mL (60mg)
    Ongoing—Morning: 60–70mL (60–70mg)Stay at lowest effective dose.

    Stimulants — Methylphenidate

    Preferred approach: start with Ritalin IR to establish effective dose, then convert to long-acting. Direct titration with long-acting is also reasonable.

    Ritalin IR (10mg tabs)
    PhaseDurationDosingNote
    Days 1–22 daysMorning: 5mg · Midday: 5mg
    Days 3–97 daysMorning: 10mg · Midday: 10mgIf tolerating, continue.
    Days 10–167 daysMorning: 20mg · Midday: 10mgIf tolerating but not fully effective, increase.
    Ongoing—Morning: 20mg · Midday: 20mgConsider afternoon 10–20mg at follow-up if needed.
    Ritalin LA (modified-release capsules)
    PhaseDurationDosingNote
    Week 17 daysMorning: 10mg
    Weeks 2–314 daysMorning: 20mgIf tolerating, continue.
    Weeks 4–514 daysMorning: 30mgIf tolerating but not fully effective, increase.
    Ongoing—Morning: 40mgAdjust at review. 60mg available if needed.
    Concerta (OROS methylphenidate) Swallow whole — never crush
    PhaseDurationDosingNote
    Week 17 daysMorning: 18mg
    Weeks 2–314 daysMorning: 27mgIf tolerating, continue.
    Weeks 4–514 daysMorning: 36mgIf tolerating but not fully effective, increase.
    Ongoing—Morning: 54mgEmpty tablet shell in stool is normal.

    Methylphenidate IR → Long-Acting Conversion

    Ritalin LA available: 10, 20, 30, 40, 60mg. Concerta available: 18, 27, 36, 54mg. * = nearest available dose — reassess at follow-up.

    Daily IR doseRitalin LAConcerta
    10mg/day10mg once daily18mg once daily
    20mg/day20mg once daily18mg once daily *
    30mg/day30mg once daily27mg once daily
    40mg/day40mg once daily36mg once daily
    50mg/day40mg once daily *54mg once daily *
    60mg/day60mg once daily54mg once daily
    80mg/day60mg once daily *54mg once daily *

    Non-Stimulants

    Atomoxetine (Strattera) Not a controlled drug — full effect takes 4–12 weeks
    PhaseDurationDosingNote
    Week 17 daysMorning: 40mgTake with food.
    Weeks 2–528 daysMorning: 80mgAllow 4 weeks at this dose before assessing effect.
    Ongoing—Morning: 80–100mgIncrease to 100mg if tolerating but not fully effective.
    Guanfacine XR (Intuniv) Must taper on cessation — do not stop abruptly
    PhaseDurationDosingNote
    Weeks 1–214 daysEvening: 1mgMonitor BP.
    Weeks 3–414 daysEvening: 2mgIf tolerating, continue increasing.
    Weeks 5–614 daysEvening: 3mg
    Ongoing—Evening: 4mgTaper gradually if stopping.
    Clonidine nocte (Catapres) Private script — must taper on cessation
    PhaseDurationDosingNote
    Day 11 dayNight: 50mcg (half tab)Expect drowsiness.
    Week 17 daysNight: 100mcg (1 tab)If tolerating, continue.
    Ongoing—Night: 100–200mcgIncrease to 200mcg if tolerating but not fully effective. Taper on cessation.
    Clonidine BD (Catapres) Private script — must taper on cessation
    PhaseDurationDosingNote
    Week 17 daysMorning: 50mcg · Afternoon: 50mcg
    Week 27 daysMorning: 100mcg · Afternoon: 100mcgIf tolerating, continue.
    Ongoing—Morning: 100mcg · Midday: 100mcg · Afternoon: 100mcgAdd midday dose if tolerating BD but not fully effective. Taper on cessation.
    💊 Titration Reference

    Reference titration schedules. Modify as needed based on clinical response and tolerability — nothing here affects the active plan.

    💵 Billing — MBS Item Numbers
    📅 Follow-Up Styles

    A reference for choosing review cadences once a patient is started on treatment. Tailor to the individual.

    Billing strategy for non-shared-care follow-ups:
    291 / 92435 (GP management plan) followed by time-based review items (300 / 302 / 304 / 306 / 308 in-person, or 91827 / 91828 / 91829 / 91830 / 91831 via video) is appropriate for titration with intention to return to GP.
    296 / 92437 (initial new-patient assessment) followed by ongoing review items is for ongoing specialist care with no immediate plan to hand back to the GP.

    Close review (2–3 weeks)

    Tighter cadence for patients who are concerning, sensitive, or anxious — e.g. ASD, OPMH, history of side-effect sensitivity, prior mental health concerns, or anyone you want to keep eyes on early.

    • First review at 2–3 weeks after commencement, then 2–3-weekly until stable
    • Tight surveillance of side effects, mood, sleep, appetite, BP/HR
    • Small dose increments (e.g. Vyvanse 30mg guided, Dex BD slow, Atomoxetine standard)
    • Move to standard cadence once tolerated and effective

    Billing: 291/92435 then 300/302/304 (or telehealth equivalents) if planning to return to GP; otherwise 296/92437 then ongoing review items.

    Standard cadence

    Default for the majority of patients without complicating factors.

    • First titration follow-up: 4–6 weeks after starting
    • Then: review every 3–5 months until clearly stable
    • Then: consider stretching to ~5-monthly
    • If stable long-term: handover of care (HOC) to GP

    Billing: 291/92435 then 300/302/304/306/308 (or telehealth equivalents) if titration plan is intended to return to GP; otherwise 296/92437 then ongoing review items.

    Go-to shared care (hands-off)

    For experienced clinicians who are comfortable with brief specialist input. Recommended once you and the patient know each other's pace.

    • Initial appointment to set up the titration plan
    • Issue the script with 5 repeats + a clear titration plan handed to the GP
    • Patient sees the GP in between for routine prescribing and monitoring
    • Specialist review at 3–6 months
    • If stable: full handover of care (HOC)

    Best paired with a thorough GP handover letter (see Handover overlay).

    Full handover to GP (HOC)

    Patient is stable, happy, and the GP is willing and able to take over ongoing prescribing under the relevant state framework. The patient remains on your books and can be re-engaged at any time.

    • Provide GP handover letter (see Handover overlay)
    • Confirm GP has applied for the relevant authority/permit/notification in their state
    • Patient knows to ask the GP to re-refer if any concerns

    Handover ≠ discharge. The therapeutic relationship remains; only the routine prescribing is delegated.

    PRN review

    Reserved for patients fully managed by their GP under shared care, where the psychiatrist is contacted only as needed. Document the trigger that would warrant re-review.

    Discharge from care

    True discharge ends the therapeutic relationship. Use only when the patient no longer requires specialist input and the GP can fully manage long-term, or when the patient relocates and is being transferred to another service.

    Send a discharge summary to the GP. Make clear how the patient can re-refer if needed.

    🤝 GP Handover — Letter & Guide
